data.title: Modiv Industrial: Monthly Dividend REIT With Potential From Tariffs And Supply Chain Shifts
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Modiv Industrial, Inc. (MDV) is the only direct-to-retail crowdfunded REIT to ever publicly list on a stock exchange, with 43 properties leased to several mission-critical tenants across industries including infrastructure, automotive, defense, industrials, government and technology.

The stock has been quite volatile since the IPO in 2022, although it’s still under pressure since the initial tariffs announcement, despite their business model of focusing on single-tenant net-lease industrial manufacturing real estate, currently presenting an opportunity to get solid monthly dividend yields.

MDV’s latest report has been solid, with $4.511 million in AFFO and an increase to $0.36 per share vs $0.34 per share a year ago. What’s much more impressive is the YTD growth, with the $13.2 million AFFO reached over the first 9 months of the year representing $1.07 per share, meaning a massive ~10.31% increase on a per share basis.

This comes in part thanks to cost savings and asset recycling that’s advancing, and we should also consider the disposition of a Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) property, whose lease expired at the end of July 2025 and is currently held for sale at a contractual value of ~$25.6 million, with an agreement with KB Home (KBH) expected to close by December 15, with the possibility of an extension to February 15. They also expect to do a parcel split and sell a Californian property formerly leased to Solar Turbines whose lease expired on September 30, 2025. These should represent very solid sources of capital, although it can take some time until they’re reflected again in the AFFO.

The company’s WALT (weighted average lease term) is also very solid, with just 1% of their leases expiring under 2 years, only ~8% over the next 2-5 years and half of them expiring in 15 years or more, so we’re looking at a very solid 14.2-year average overall, with ~2.5% average annual rent increases. They even expect this WALT to grow as they recycle assets, so it's definitely a very big plus.

Financially, based on MDV’s latest report, we see a normal position for a REIT, without a lot of cash available but with significantly more assets than liabilities, with a normal amount of leverage. Even when we exclude the noncontrolling interests, the current market cap is not far from their ~$164.82 million in equity, and we should keep in mind that we’re talking about assets that likely went up in value since being recorded on the balance sheet, not depreciated.

It’s also important to note that their $250 million term loan (most of their debt) is due in 2027, so they have a bit of time to wait for rate cuts to advance before having to refinance—potentially splitting this into more loans and spreading it over more time.

They also failed to approve a reverse split of anywhere between 1:500 to 1:1,500, which was supposed to be followed by a forward split (these do not include their preferred (MDV.PR.A) 7.375% Series A stock, currently paying less than the common stock). The CEO detailed the reasoning behind it very well in this news release, basically saying that this was supposed to be just an administrative function intended to reduce costs and potentially increase liquidity.

This is something that goes back to the company’s crowdfunding era, and it’s one of the reasons the CEO even joined in 2018. As you probably guessed from this, one of the main reasons behind this move was to cut costs with the transfer agent, as the company had nearly 10,000 separate accounts when going public, compared to a handful that a normal company does (brokers acting as intermediaries), as they got a lot of people to join the crowdfunding initially. This generates expenses in the six-digit range every year, and it’s an interesting thing to remember about smaller companies that’s often ignored.

At the moment the release was published, the company had about 4,000 accounts at their transfer agent, of which about 3,500 of these held less than 1,500 shares (a lot under 500 even), meaning a lot of unnecessary costs since they aren’t allowed to cash out accounts with more than 1 share. What this reverse split was supposed to do is allow them to cash out these “dormant” accounts that still didn’t move to a broker (basically buy out some of the people who bought during the crowdfunding and perhaps even forgot about the account), eliminate unnecessary costs, and then do a split back to normal share prices.

Unfortunately, the measure missed the necessary threshold by a small margin, despite a wide majority of the people who voted being in favor of it (not enough votes overall), but maybe another try could work next time.

Dividends & Buybacks

Modiv pays a monthly dividend (as the name also hints at) of $0.0975 per share, for a yield of roughly 8% today, which is among their highest in quite some time. This should cost them about $14.84 million based on the current number of shares, meaning a roughly 84.32% payout ratio on the common dividends based on an annualized AFFO of $17.6 million.

On March 4, 2025, Modiv also authorized a buyback program for its Series A preferred stock, and through September 30, 2025, it repurchased a total of 275,000 shares, meaning ~13.8% of the preferred shares issued, for a total of $6.5 million at an average cost of $23.74 per share.

Risks & Opportunities

One big opportunity to keep in mind for Modiv actually comes from Trump’s tariffs. As I mentioned before, I see them as a tool to get leverage in near/medium-term negotiations rather than a long-term norm, with the $750 billion LNG deal with the EU that came from them being a perfect example. However, another effect I expect is reinforcing the US’s internal supply chains, as companies would seek to prevent/avoid these tariffs and focus on local logistics and sourcing rather than relying on other countries.

Although the market initially panic-sold this like any other stock back in early April when tariffs were officially announced, the stock quickly jumped to nearly $17 later on the same month, although it retreated quickly after. Owning this kind of mission-critical, niche/specialized buildings that is essential for the supply chain is ultimately what the company does, and as companies bring back their production capacity inside the country, more opportunities for MDV should arise.

But again, this relative weakness we’ve seen for REITs is ultimately normal in this environment. As a REIT, the company depends on external capital to expand since they have to pay most of what they make back to us. As we can see, Modiv has maintained their debt around the same level since 2023, although they issued some stock back in early and late 2024 to fund some acquisitions with initial cap rates of ~8%. This is key for REITs since they don’t have that many sources of capital except for issuing stock, issuing debt or selling assets to acquire others, and weakness should be expected when the stock is trading at unattractive multiples and debt costs are high.

In fact, this external capital even goes back to the company’s inception, when they started raising capital from individual investors back in 2012 through crowdfunding.

This is why rate cuts will also be an essential catalyst for this asset class’ recovery. Not only would they make debt costs cheaper and encourage higher investments, but they could also boost the economy by increasing discretionary budgets, helping Modiv’s tenants and making the overall investment more attractive.

To simplify this, think about how the company would fund an investment with an initially expected 8% cap rate (normal in the industry, similar deals seen recently). They can issue stock, but that also costs them 8% per year (the current yield), while debt could be a bit cheaper but not significantly better due to the high interest rates affecting the SOFR rate, so they don’t have that many options to efficiently develop, unlike a low-rate environment where REITs trade at lower yields and debt is cheaper. This is a key reason why the asset class is not very attractive for the market during bad times, although I’d argue that this is the best time to consolidate a position at great yields.

On top of that, they can lower treasury yields given enough time, assuming that nothing fundamental changes for the US that would warrant borrowing at higher costs. As the theoretical risk-free rate would then fall, the market would be “forced” to move into better risk-adjusted yields, with Modiv being a solid contender for that especially if their cost of debt would fall as well. Even recently, the yield on the 10-year treasury fell below 4% but then jumped to nearly 4.2% due to the uncertainty around the next cuts before falling to the 4.04% seen today as the market’s fear slowed down a bit.

But again, these things are not instant. The correlation between rates and 10-year treasuries can be seen with the naked eye, but we can also notice the significant near-term volatility, so don’t expect this to solve everything overnight. I say that especially after the US Government went through its longest shutdown in history, which made the near-term future of these cuts questionable by the market, although some Fed officials signaled they see the possibility of a December cut—but we can’t be sure until it happens.

The CEO actually had a nice comment regarding these delayed effects and broader market moves during the Q3 Earnings Release:

But if we start to see real easing and we start to see some consistent trends for REITs, I don't know if that means we need a consolidation on the sort of the rest of the S&P and NASDAQ to get that or not, it's hard to say because you could argue that until tech and some of these names cool off, then no one is really going to ever consider boring REITs. But at the same time, if they force correct, is that just going to drag everyone back down. So it remains to see, it's pretty cloudy. But even despite that cloudiness, I feel pretty optimistic about what I'm seeing.

Another near-term catalyst in this regard could come from the new Fed Chair, with Powell’s term ending in May 2026 and Trump likely naming a more obedient Chair this time. Sure, that’s just one vote (the Federal Open Market Committee is composed of 12 votes), but as we’ve seen, especially in recent years, the Chair remains a very important spokesperson for the market, with immense influence that can give the market a lot of fear or confidence. I also later saw that the CEO even mentioned something regarding this during their Q3 Earnings Release, noting how the company has been public during quite a terrible environment, I might add.

If you think about the timing of this company’s IPO, we had the war in Ukraine coming a few days after, followed by rate hikes, so we haven’t seen them trade in a “normal” environment so far, with the stock being pressured by macro factors for basically all of its short existence.

It’s also worth noting that the CEO has a good history of mergers and acquisitions, and Modiv itself could be part of either of those, offering a solid portfolio of companies that could attract a potential buyer wanting to diversify while also trading at a size that allows them to carefully pick every single property they invest in—an advantage over larger companies that buy entire portfolios at once in billion-dollar transactions.

So, even though the long-term should look quite good for Modiv and its asset class, we should be aware that the near-term will likely continue to be volatile, which is not great for a company with inherently quite limited liquidity. This is why the management will have to continue being careful with their capital allocation, recycling, and issuing. Although I’d say they've done a great job so far, being such a small REIT also means that mistakes or tenant issues can be very costly, with a lot of dependency on California, a small property count, and even fewer tenants.

Valuation

Starting with an annualized AFFO of $17.6 million in 2025, I believe we can see them deliver a relatively solid CAGR of at least 7.5% over the first 5 years thanks to asset recycling moves, potential cost savings, rate cuts lowering interest expenses, followed by a 4.5% CAGR as the company still has a lot of room to develop, with a 3% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a WACC of 8% (above my estimated 7.88% based on an ~8.5% cost of equity, ~7.5% cost of debt), $269.8 million in net debt, and 12.686 million shares and units outstanding as of the Q3 report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $447.04 million, an equity value of $177.24 million, and an intrinsic value of $13.97 per share, which is almost identical to the current price.

Conclusions

I rate MDV a Buy thanks to their high-yield monthly dividends and potential recovery in an improving environment, with solid long-term growth potentially coming from macro tailwinds started by tariffs. Although MDV trades around a fair value based on my calculations, we’re talking about a fair value for a relatively high expected yield, while rate cuts should push these expectations lower and likely result in higher stock prices. Risks remain, especially due to their tenant concentration and property count, but that also comes with higher growth potential compared to REITs that are already virtually omnipresent.

data.title: Q3 2025 Insights From Gabelli Value Portfolio Managers
data.description:
Amid continued geopolitical tensions, inflation concerns, a still uncertain global trade environment, and a looming U.S. government shutdown at quarter-end, equity markets continued their seemingly inexorable climb in the third quarter. The S&P 500 Index rose 8.1% and small caps, which have long lagged the overall market, rose even more, with the Russell 2000 Index climbing 12.4%.

Artificial intelligence continues to be the primary driver of market returns, with most of the “Magnificent Seven” at or near all-time highs. That said, this quarter’s market rally has broadened, with cyclicals such as industrials and financials also contributing to the rally.

We wrote in our second quarter letter about the “Three T’s”: Trade, Taxes, and Trust. Trade continues to be in flux, with the U.S. signing deals with many partners (including the UK and European Union), though uncertainty remains for many significant countries, including China and India. Tax cuts were mostly extended in the One Big Beautiful Bill signed in July, which also provided additional stimulus with accelerated depreciation. But trust remains shaky, with an unpredictable administration, $37 trillion in national debt, and a $2 trillion annual budget deficit, leading gold and bitcoin to extend their rallies at nearly $4,000 per ounce and $120,000, respectively, as of this writing.

Another “T” we would add this quarter is “Treasuries.” The Federal Reserve cut rates for the first time this year in September, lowering the Fed Funds rate 25 bps to 4%-4.25%, with expectations for further cuts to come. Those additional cuts depend on the economy, inflation, and unemployment. Second quarter GDP grew a robust 3.8%, with estimates for third quarter GDP growth in the 1.5%-2% range. Inflation is well below its recent peak, though it actually rose to 2.9% in August from 2.7% in June and July – all still above the Fed’s 2% target. Unemployment, while still low at 4.3%, has also ticked up in recent months. 10-Year U.S. Treasury yields declined during the quarter and are currently around 4.1%. All things being equal (which they never are), lower rates should be good for equity prices as they support higher multiples and encourage investment and deal-making.

Deals, Deals & More Deals

Despite a lull around “Liberation Day” due to tariff uncertainty, the merger & acquisition boom expected under President Trump has come to fruition so far in 2025, with global deals up 33% year-to-date to $3 trillion, a four-year high. Private equity-backed M&A is up 27%. Continued lower interest rates, greater trade certainty, lower regulatory scrutiny, and a growing economy should further bolster deal activity.

Conclusion

Despite uncertainty on many macroeconomic and geopolitical fronts, there are some clear tailwinds for equities: easing trade tensions, lower rates, a lighter touch on regulation, and stimulative policies. That said, the market isn’t “cheap” at nearly 30x 2025 earnings. We continue to seek unappreciated and undervalued companies – often in the overlooked small to mid-cap area – to find quality franchise companies with catalysts that can surface value. Despite the market rally, opportunities do abound, and our team of portfolio managers and analysts continue to use our bottom-up process and Private Market Value with a Catalyst philosophy to find them.

Notes and Important Information

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of a Fund before investing.

A Fund’s NAV per share will fluctuate with changes in the market value of the Fund’s portfolio securities. Stocks are subject to market, economic, and business risks that cause their prices to fluctuate. A Fund’s use of leverage may magnify the volatility of net asset value changes versus Funds that do not employ leverage. When Fund shares are sold, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Consequently, you can lose money by investing in a Fund.

The Net Asset Value per share appears in the Publicly Traded Funds column in Monday’s The Wall Street Journal. It is also listed in Barron’s Mutual Funds/Closed-End Funds section.

The Net Asset Value per share may be obtained each day by calling (914) 921-5070 or visiting our website.

Information regarding a Fund’s distribution policy and the most recent shareholder report, which contains a more extensive list of holdings, is available by calling 1-800-GABELLI (1-800-422-3554) or visiting our website.

Premium/Discount Discussion

As a refresher for our shareholders, the price of a closed-end fund is determined in the open market by willing buyers and sellers. Closed-End Fund shares trade on an exchange and may trade at a premium to (higher than) net asset value (the market value of the Fund’s underlying portfolio and other assets less any liabilities) or a discount to (lower than) net asset value. Ideally, a Fund’s market price will generally track the NAV. However, a Fund’s premium or discount to NAV may vary over time.

Keeping You Informed With Every Step

(OTCQX: GAMI) is widely recognized for its research-driven, value-oriented investment process based on the principles first articulated in 1934 by the fathers of modern security analysis, Graham and Dodd, and further augmented by Mario Gabelli with his introduction of the concept of Private Market Value (PMV) with a Catalyst TM into security analysis.

data.title: NVYY: Nvidia With A High Distribution And Great Structuring
data.description:
Thesis

2025 has seen the advent of single-name ETFs, which have flooded the market. Single-name ETFs achieve the goal of constantly harvesting gains from the underlying stock and generally are structured on top of equities that have seen a boom in their market value. The funds attract investors via their high distributions, but not all are created equal. We have covered a number of them, some with better structuring than others:

  • PLTW: Let Palantir Pay Your Weekly Bills
  • SMCY: High Risk, Low Reward

In today's article we are going to analyze another single-name ETF, namely the GraniteShares YieldBOOST NVDA ETF (NVYY).

What does the fund do?

Let us start by looking at the fund objective as per its website:

The GraniteShares YieldBOOST NVDA ETF (“NVYY”) is designed to generate income through options strategies, primarily by selling put options on leveraged ETFs that track the 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF.

The ETF therefore employs a fairly straightforward strategy - sell puts on the GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF (NVDL). If NVDL remains at the same price or moves higher, NVYY makes the option premium. Conversely, if NVDL moves lower, the ETF will incur a loss by cash settling the difference upon option expiry. The current composition is as follows:

The fund has sold November 26, 2025 puts; November 28, 2025 puts; and December 1, 2025 options. We can see from the above table that, in fact, the ETF has done its trade via put spreads rather than outright puts. This simply means that the downside is more limited. Let us have a closer look:

  • The ETF is short a Nov 26 put with a 90.34 strike.
  • The ETF is long a Nov 26 put with an 81.31 strike.

The maximum loss from this structure is the difference in the two strikes (i.e., 90.34 minus 81.31), rather than the entire figure from 90.34 to zero. Put spreads are more conservative ways to express the same view, but they also come with a lower net option premium.

Performance

The ETF is fairly new, but we can still get a sense of its performance so far:

The ETF was issued in May 2025, so our graph starts then. Please note we are using a total return graph here in order to account for the high distributions that the ETF disburses. We can see that NVYY (the teal line) has a total return profile that very much resembles a pure long position in Nvidia. That is a very good result, and speaks very well to the portfolio management team.

Please note that the ETF is structured so that its net delta positioning more closely resembles an outright long rather than the leveraged NVDL fund. We can clearly see that during downturns, when the blue line (NVDL) has very significant drawdowns versus NVYY. The fund does a very good job at harvesting the implied volatility associated with NVDL.

We can also see that Granite Shares did a good job for their structuring by comparing NVYY with the YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY):

The two funds are highly correlated, and ultimately produce a very similar total return on the analyzed time-frame.

Very high distributions

As per its stated goal, the ETF pays out a very high distribution:

We can see that the November 2025 distribution rate is 101.82%, which is an astoundingly high figure. An investor can access the entire distribution history here, on the Seeking Alpha platform.

Please remember that by design, single-name ETFs will have a high distribution, but one should never look at the dividend figures in isolation. The best visual here is the total return graph that outlines what the ETF produced on a net basis (and that figure is roughly 32% since inception). What ends up happening in a bull market with single name funds is that investors get to 'amortize' their initial investment quite fast. Let us look at the price performance for the name:

From a pure price perspective, the ETF is down -18% since inception, but its total return provides for a net gain of 32%. This constant gains harvesting means that during a theoretical bear market where Nvidia would crash, an investor would be principal protected since they would have already received in cash a large portion of their investment. What do we mean? Well, if we annualize the total return so far, we get a figure of roughly 60%. This means that if Nvidia continues to perform the same way, an investor would fully amortize an investment here via NVYY in roughly 1.5 years (i.e., 100% total return). The remaining outstanding amount in the ETF would therefore represent a 'zero cost' position going forward.

Risk factors

The main risk factor for this ETF is the performance of the underlying stock, Nvidia (NVDA). As long as NVDA goes up in value or stays constant, NVYY will deliver. In fact, via its structuring, NVYY is set to outperform during times when Nvidia is range-bound. Since NVYY sells puts on a high volatility name, it will harvest the implied volatility and generate a high total return even when the underlying stock is range-bound.

A significant recession that would see a plunge in value for NVDA would also negatively impact NVYY. As we saw from the analysis above, the downside is very similar for the two securities. Do expect very significant drawdowns for NVYY in a recession, basically matching the total return for the underlying name Nvidia.

The second risk factor to consider is the volatility drag associated with a leveraged fund like NVDL. When the market is very choppy, the volatility drag effect can cause names like NVDL to underperform, thus potentially affecting the put spreads written on the fund.

Conclusion

NVYY is a single-name exchange-traded fund. The ETF looks to constantly harvest gains from the Nvidia stock via weekly distributions. The fund is structured via put writing on a 2x levered name, but has a similar total return performance as outright Nvidia. We like the name and its structuring for investors looking to be long Nvidia while constantly taking gains. A recession would see a downside in the ETF very similar to Nvidia outright.

data.title: BlackRock Global Allocation Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 09/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 4.51% (Institutional shares) and 4.48% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the third quarter of 2025, while its reference benchmark[4] returned [36%].
  • The fund had an overweight allocation to equities (4%) and an underweight position in fixed income (-10%), while commodity-related securities (4%) and cash equivalents (2%) accounted for the rest. In equities, it had overweight holdings in the communication services, consumer discretionary, information technology (IT), and energy sectors, and underweight positions in the consumer staples, real estate, and materials sectors. The fund was overweight the United States and underweight Japan, Canada, Australia, and Europe.
  • In fixed income, it had an underweight exposure to developed market government bonds and overweight allocations to corporate credit, securitized debt, and bank loans. Total portfolio duration (interest rate sensitivity) was modestly above the benchmark at 2.4 years (total portfolio duration assumes equity duration of 0). From a currency perspective, the fund had an overweight U.S. dollar position and underweight euro exposure.
Contributors Detractors
In equities, security selection in the energy sector and an underweight position and security selection in the consumer staples sector positively impacted returns. In fixed income, corporate credit exposure, specifically high yield and loans, was additive. The allocation to commodities, notably gold-related securities, also contributed. In equities, security selection in IT and consumer staples, which was partially offset by overweight exposures to the sectors, dragged on performance. Security selection and an overweight position in the health care sector detracted, along with security selection in the industrials and financials sectors. The cash and cash equivalent holding also weighed on returns.

Further insight

Global stocks advanced during the quarter, led by Chinese equities, which experienced gains across artificial intelligence (AI) and social media-related companies. Beyond China, equity markets were led by U.S. small-cap stocks, which benefited from an expectation of easing monetary conditions. From a sector perspective, IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary were the strongest performers, as they have significant direct exposure to AI-related themes. Similarly, optimism about AI drove growth’s outperformance of value. “Defensive” market segments generally lagged, including real estate investment trusts and the consumer staples and health care sectors. The Federal Reserve’s (Fed) rate cut supported nearly all major fixed income sectors, with longer-dated maturities generally outperforming shorter-term bonds. Emerging market bonds performed strongly, as looser monetary policy from the Fed eased liquidity concerns and boosted demand for higher-yielding issuers. Lastly, U.S. credit, both investment grade and high yield, enjoyed solid relative performance due to the resumption of the Fed’s rate cuts and the U.S. economy’s underlying strength.

data.title: ClearBridge Dividend Strategy Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The third quarter continued the post-Liberation Day rebound for U.S. equity markets, with animal spirits driving the S&P 500 Index to record highs. Investor optimism was fueled by better-than-feared tariff outcomes, the passing of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July, anticipated interest rate cuts — the Federal Reserve delivered with a 25 bps cut in September and signaled further easing — and robust corporate earnings.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the industrials, IT, health care and utilities sectors proved beneficial.
  • Detractors: An underweight to IT and an overweight to consumer staples detracted. Stock selection in the communication services and real estate sectors also detracted.
  • Outlook: With investors continuing to be complacent in the face of historically high valuations, we continue to emphasize investments with more modest valuations. The gap between the valuation of our portfolio and the market remains as wide as it has ever been. We believe this positions us both for better potential appreciation and lower downside risk.

Performance Review

  • AI enthusiasm continued to propel the S&P 500 Index higher, as evidenced by sizable gains in the information technology sector as well as tech proxies in the communication services and consumer discretionary sectors. The Fund profited from its exposure to AI, though we lagged the index as our dividend mandate and our disciplined adherence to risk management has us underweight the IT sector.
  • During the quarter, we benefited from our positions in Broadcom (AVGO) and Oracle (ORCL), both of which are major players in AI. Both stocks surged in September on robust earnings and outlooks.
  • TE Connectivity (TEL) was a strong contributor on the strength of its AI-enabled sensors and embedded systems.
  • Defense holdings Northrop Grumman (NOC), RTX (RTX) and recent addition L3Harris Technologies (LHX) performed well in an environment of heightened geopolitical tensions.
  • Sempra’s recent moves to focus on its regulated U.S. utilities businesses and strengthen its overall financial position spurred its shares higher.
  • Consumer staples such as Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGF)(OTCPK:NSRGY) and Coca-Cola (KO) took a back seat in a risk-on market, as did Comcast (CMCSA), due to heightened competitive pressures in broadband.
  • From a sector perspective, our overweights to consumer staples, real estate and materials all detracted from performance.

Outlook

As we head into the final quarter of the year, our outlook is mixed. The economy remains resilient and looks likely to muddle through, provided the government shutdown is not prolonged. Valuations, however, are on the fuller side, restraining the outlook for capital appreciation. With investors continuing to be complacent in the face of historically high valuations, we continue to emphasize investments with more modest valuations. The gap between the valuation of our portfolio and the market remains as wide as it has ever been. We believe this positions us both for better potential appreciation and lower downside risk. The combination of higher tariffs, a weaker dollar and a smaller labor pool increases the risk that inflation will remain higher for longer than investors expect. Should inflation prove sticky, dividend growth will be a boon to investors. We believe dividend growers are the perfect all-weather investment. Dividends cushion the bumps of volatile markets, provide much-needed income in low-return environments, and preserve and grow purchasing power in inflationary environments. We believe our companies are well-positioned to deliver continued increases, and we feel good about our holdings.

data.title: American Express: Its Good Run Doesn't End Here
data.description:
As inflation remains sticky and stubborn, consumers may have a hard time maintaining their spending and budget. This can be a double-edged sword for many companies involved in consumer finance because inflation can create demand and recession woes at the same time. With that, many large players capitalize on their established brand recognition, vast market presence, and solid customer base. A perfect example is American Express Company (AXP) with its robust performance and well-positioned balance sheet. So even if the stock price has already tripled since 2020, valuation remains justifiable. Technicals adhere as bullish signals persist.

AXP Q3 2025: Robust And Resilient To Macroeconomic Headwinds

In recent months, inflation has proven its stubbornness. Although it has already weakened from skyrocketing levels in 2022-2023, prices are still increasing. Even so, it continues to open new opportunities for consumer finance companies. A big player like American Express Company largely benefits from these trends as more consumers and merchants open themselves to credit cards. We can see it in its most recent performance.

During Q3 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $18.4B, up by 10.8% YoY from $16.6B. Both its interest and non-interest segments rose YoY, showing its strong performance amid macroeconomic volatility. Discount revenue remained the biggest revenue component, as it comprised 51% of the total after deducting the interest expense. This reflected the strong closed-loop network of Amex. This should not be surprising in the current market landscape, supported by the digital revolution, which has increased access and preference for cashless payments. I will discuss this more later. More consumers spending using Amex and merchants accepting its cards strengthened it. Meanwhile, interest on loans rose by 9.8% despite the lower interest rate following the policy easing cycle in the second half of 2024. It also benefited from the interest rate cut as the interest expense decreased.

Moreover, AXP maintained efficiency despite its expanding customer and loan base. The total expenses increased by 10.2%. So, revenue growth completely offset it. Indeed, its increased operating capacity resulted in more revenues than expenses. Hence, its net income margin remained a double-digit number at 15.7% versus 15.1% YoY.

Challenges

Inflation is a double-edged sword because it can lower consumer spending. Now, it’s back to its 14-month high of 3.0%, which can affect their purchasing power more. This can be aggravated by new tariffs because these raise import costs, which are typically passed on to consumers. With that, it must think of ways to maintain high retention rates of Amex users. In addition, competition with other large players like Visa (V) (VISA:CA) and Mastercard (MA) (MA:CA) must also be considered.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these challenges, I believe that AXP maintains a strong market position.

One aspect to consider is its business model itself. Unlike V and MA, Amex acts as both a card issuer and a bank network, allowing it to keep the card fees and/or discount revenues for itself. It doesn’t have to share these with banks. It partners with other banks, but mostly for co-branded cards. This allows it to expand even in other countries while retaining a large portion of fees and discount revenues for itself. For instance, Amex partners with Banco de Oro, or BDO in the Philippines. Since I have a bank account in BDO, I also received an Amex card from this bank. Aside from that, Amex has a large loan base, merchant network, and liquidity, which allows it to be self-sufficient and cover its expanding customer base.

A robust balance sheet is another core strength of the company. As a consumer-finance business, card member loans comprise a substantial portion of its assets. But it is worth noting that it has enough liquid assets. The combined amount of cash, deposits, and short-term investments is equivalent to almost 20% of the total assets. It also has card member receivables, which are typically credit card balances due in less than a year. This comprises 20% of the total assets. If there are delinquencies or even uncollected receivables, AXP can sell these receivables and loans. Lastly, its net debt/EBITDA is only 0.28x. This means that AXP only needs a quarter to cover all debt at once, even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, it has enough capital buffer to sustain its expanding operating capacity and dividends.

It may also benefit from the combined impact of stubborn inflation and the recent policy easing cycle. Inflation may weaken purchasing power if income can’t cope with the rising prices. But it may increase their reliance on credit cards to support their spending. In a recent survey, it was found that three out of four Americans own credit cards. This can be supported by the recent policy easing cycle, which has slashed the interest rate by 50 bps to 4.0%. This may lower credit card loan yields since these are typically variable-rate loans. But this can lead to a larger customer and loan base, especially this holiday season.

Moreover, the digital revolution can have some positive spillovers since it has made the internet more accessible in recent years, spurred by the events during the pandemic. This led to the rise of a cashless society. Credit cards are already a staple for many. Providers like AXP partner with e-wallets across the globe to expand their market coverage and penetrate more potential customers.

Valuation

If we use price ratios, AXP will appear overpriced at first glance. For instance, P/E shows that the stock trades at 23.92x its EPS versus the five-year average of 18.31x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price, will be $272.42. The same applies to P/B because the stock trades at 7.55x its book value per share versus the historical average of 4.75x. This leads to a TP of $223.73. Lastly, P/S using sales per share suggests a TP of $241.52.

All three price ratios show that AXP has already increased more than necessary. While the market warranted an uptrend, the stock price rose faster and further than the 2020-2024 average. However, that doesn’t automatically tell us that AXP is expensive or overvalued. After all, price ratios only show how expensive or cheap the stock is relative to previous years and may not indicate the intrinsic value. The thing is, the market does not care much about whether the stock trades above or below historical levels. As long as these financial metrics increase, the price will most likely increase as well.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. I based its annualized dividend on the quarterly payout of $0.82. Meanwhile, the dividend growth rate is the CAGR since 2021. This is to disregard the impact or any inconsistency caused by the pandemic. This is also more conservative since the rate of 12.07% is lower than the 2020-2025 CAGR of 12.94%. That way, we can avoid overvaluing the stock. I also set its risk premium at 7.0% instead of the standard of 5.0% to price in more risks. Lastly, I estimated beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function of the daily price of AXP versus the S&P 500. This means that AXP has been more volatile than the index. Using all these numbers, the TP will be $420.51. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will become $378.46. This proves that AXP is still trading at a reasonable level, so upside potential is still possible.

Technicals

AXP’s technicals are still bullish despite the selloff last week. The stock price is still moving above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which suggests that the uptrend still holds. It also means that the current price trend is still stronger than the average in the past 50 or 200 days. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, so market sentiments are still optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI reflect the recent price weakness after the selloff. The MACD line is still below the signal line, while the histogram is within negative territory. This implies that selling volume has outpaced buying volume in the past two weeks. But their shifting trend a few days ago could tell us that buyers found a new support level or entry point after the dip. This can tell us that buying volume is starting to rebound. Lastly, RSI shows that the dip was due to profit-taking after the stock became oversold. Now, the stock moves at a normal range, so buying room has expanded again.

Conclusion

American Express Company remains robust with its strategic business, solid customer base, and stable cost and expense management. This is supported by its well-positioned balance sheet and the recent policy easing cycle. Its valuation also remains attractive and justifies further upside even if it trades above historical averages. Technicals adhere to it as buying momentum rebounds after the recent dip. I am giving this stock a buy rating.

data.title: Thrivent Short-Term Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance factors

Thrivent Short-Term Bond Fund outperformed the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit 1-3 Year Bond Index by 0.28% during the third quarter. This outperformance was largely attributable to an overweight to risk assets and security selection as spreads continued to grind tighter following the significant tariff-induced volatility of 2Q. Over the quarter, we reduced our exposure to corporates as spreads tightened, conducting outright sales and swaps in order to take advantage of corporate new issue concessions. On the securitized side, we increased our allocation to Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS) and Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) during the quarter, while lowering our allocation to Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) due to their floating rate coupons. Our longer duration also contributed to performance as rates rallied over the quarter, with the 2-year Treasury declining 11 basis points to end at 3.61%.

Over the 12-month period, the Fund outperformed the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit 1-3 Year Bond Index by 0.93%. The most significant driver of the Fund’s outperformance was our significant overweight to risk assets, which offered higher carry over the time period, as well as 1-3 year spreads that tightened by 10 bps. Notably, the 2-year Treasury rate ended the 12-month period at a very similar level to where it began, around 3.60%, belying the volatility experienced over the 12 months as the market tried to price in the Fed’s potential course of action.

The Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit 1-3 Year Bond Index is composed of 72% U.S. Treasuries, 5% government-related securities and 23% investment-grade corporates. The Fund currently holds 62% of its assets in corporate bonds, down from 66% at end of 2Q, and its holdings have an average ratings quality that is somewhat lower than the index but still investment-grade rated. In addition, the Fund owns 30% securitized debt. Over longer periods of time, overweights to these asset classes provide more yield, while the short-duration nature of the Fund’s holdings offer attractive breakeven levels during spread-widening scenarios.

Portfolio outlook

In September, the Fed Funds rate was cut to 4-4.25% from the 4.25-4.5% range, as the Fed judged that downside risks to employment had risen and outweighed risks of elevated inflation. Notably, the unemployment rate rose to 4.3%, the highest since 2021, and nonfarm payrolls as well as ADP payrolls have come in significantly below expectations. September's ADP report of -32k jobs was the largest decline in 2.5 years and was coupled with a revision in August from +54k to -3k, so it seems that the Fed's concerns were warranted. Inflation remains elevated as the Fed's projection for core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index, the Fed's preferred measure, remains at 3.1% for 2025; however, commentary suggests the Fed expects inflation to slowly moderate over the next few years and gradually return to its 2% target by 2028. The market is pricing in two additional 25 bps rate cuts this year as risks to employment are expected to continue to win the tug-of-war of between the competing objectives of the Fed's dual mandate. Given our current outlook for short-term rates, we are likely to continue to manage the Fund to a slightly longer duration than the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit 1-3 Year Bond Index.

In the corporate market, 1-3 year spreads are at the tightest levels of the post-ultra-low-rate environment that began when the Fed started raising rates in early 2022. Spreads remain 15 bps wide of the all-time tights achieved in 2021, indicating that they could continue to perform if economic conditions remain favorable. However, with less cushion for potential economic weakness or policy surprises, we are favoring up-in-quality credits and shorter maturities. While the impact of tariffs has been relatively benign for corporations thus far, the economic picture of policy is not fully known, and thus we believe it prudent to leave some dry powder to take advantage of potential market volatility. On the securitized side, with the expectation of tariff-induced inflation, the consumer will continue to see their dollar stretched and therefore could see stress. Furthermore, although housing remains strong, new home starts have slowed, inventories have crept higher, and mortgage rates remain elevated which has translated to lower affordability. Overall, we will continue to manage the Fund with the objective of maintaining sufficient carry while leaving room to add risk when markets offer better entry points.

data.title: Soluna Holdings: A Flourishing Green Data Center Business
data.description:
Thesis

Soluna Holdings, Inc. is a digital infrastructure company that develops green data center projects near renewable energy parks designed to power AI, HPC, and Bitcoin operations. There are projects already operating and others under development, with a long-term total capacity target exceeding 1 GW. The company has already demonstrated its capability through its operating projects. Given the high growth anticipated from future projects, the booming data center ecosystem, and its current market capitalization, there is potential for a significantly higher valuation and return on invested capital in the future. Despite the buy thesis, though, certain risks should be considered, along with a cautious approach.

Company Overview

Soluna Holdings, Inc. (SLNH), commenced trading on the Nasdaq under the ticker SLNH on March 23, 2021, after rebranding and M&A activities of Mechanical Technology Inc. The stock took part in the 2021 everything bubble and then plummeted to $0.40 until last September. The market has been showing its love for data center-related stocks since then, and SLNH was no exception. However, the stock pulled back significantly from its recent high of $4.42 to $1.51 now.

The company develops green projects to capitalize on the data center megatrend, focusing on bitcoin mining and hosting, as well as AI and HPC. The concept is to build data centers near renewable energy parks to utilize surplus energy that would otherwise be wasted, thereby exploiting relatively low-cost, reliable energy sources.

Projects

The company has its projects named after prominent women scientists. Soluna commenced mining operations in 2021 at Murray (Project Sophie, 25MW) and Calvert City (Project Marie, 25MW), Kentucky. The company exited Project Marie in Q1 2023 and shifted Project Sophie towards hosting in Q2 2023. Three projects were operating; now there are four, with the completion of Dorothy 2 on November 13, 2025. Total operational capacity is 123 MW. In addition, Soluna has its 166MW Kati project ready for construction, and seven projects are under development, totaling over 1 GW of potential capacity. Spring Lane Capital and Navitas Global are major Soluna partners, providing funds and investing in certain projects. Soluna owns 100% of Project Sophie, 15% of Project Dorothy 1A, 51% of Project Dorothy 1B, and 15% (that can reach 49% under certain conditions) of Project Dorothy 2. The projects under development are flexible to support BTC Hosting and/or AI, indicating that Soluna acknowledged the increased impact of AI on the data center ecosystem.

Soluna is riding two modern megatrends; renewable energy and AI are booming this decade and will continue to do so for the foreseeable future. For instance, Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) has recently announced plans to invest $40 billion in three new data centers in Texas. Data center development is here to stay, and Soluna will undoubtedly capitalize on this fact.

Q3 2025 Earnings Report & Business Update

On November 17, 2025, Soluna announced its Q3 earnings report. Revenue for Q3 2025 was $8.4MM. Gross profit margin reached 28%, 9% higher than Q2 2025. Adjusted EBITDA decreased by $2.9MM, compared to Q3 2024 reaching -$6.4MM. Excluding special charges from Cloudco HPE costs, bonuses, Preferred B consent fees, and broker fees, adjusted EBITDA stood at $0.1MM. The company holds $60.5 million in cash reserves, and debt stands at $23 million.

Although Soluna is still in an early stage, it already has projects operating, which is important for reducing investment risk. In addition, the company is executing on its commitment to diversify from mining and focus on hosting, including AI, for its under-development projects. Its financial strategy enables future growth, with the collaboration of Generate Capital, Spring Lane Capital, and Navitas. The company should be considered well-capitalized and capable of executing its future projects, given its current financial condition and the forecast of positive EBITDA in 2026.

Among recent developments, on October 6, 2025, the company regained compliance with the Nasdaq listing requirements. Furthermore, the partnership with Galaxy Digital was expanded; on November 20, CEO John Belizaire posted on his "X" account about the deployment of Galaxy containers at Project Kati 1 while Project Dorothy 2 reached full capacity. Soluna projects reached a total operational capacity of 123MW.

Valuation

Soluna is currently trading at a $103 million market cap. 2025 revenue is expected to be $32 million. This results in a ~3.2 P/S 2025 ratio, almost even to the sector median. The P/S TTM ratio is 0.75, compared to a sector median of 3.25, suggesting undervaluation. The market seems to be only considering the slight revenue reduction year over year, but not Soluna's shift from BTC mining to BTC hosting and AI data center development. In addition, certain peers, as Cipher Mining (CIFR), have significantly higher respective ratios, reflecting the industry's potential.

The company is poised for significant revenue growth in the coming years as its projects will gradually be completed, eventually reaching the 1GW+ target. Its base-case forecast for FY 2028 is $132.8 million in revenue and $54.7 million in adjusted EBITDA. Applying a 2028 P/S ratio of 5 would result in a $664MM valuation. Applying a 2028 EV/EBITDA ratio of 10 would result in $547MM. These numbers should be considered conservative, as they are based on the company's base-case forecast and conservative ratio estimates. In any case, Soluna should be expected to reach a significantly higher valuation in the coming years, even with these assumptions.

Risks

The company appears capable of landing its projects; however, future execution should not be taken for granted, as well as other factors such as partnerships, supply chain, deployment costs, and regulatory developments that could negatively impact Soluna.

Soluna is a small business, and its aspiration to develop data centers with a capacity of over 1GW requires capital. The management could seek additional funds through debt, stock issuance, or offering stakes in its projects to investing partners.

The company is at an emerging growth stage, having recently undergone restructuring; thus, its valuation will be dependent on general trends, project development announcements, and funding.

The data center ecosystem must feed a hungry AI beast. Despite the huge demand, this fact is expected to attract many peers that will try to capture a market share and compete by pushing prices lower.

Bitcoin volatility and its recent correction, if it continues, could affect Soluna, as its projects include mining and hosting. A troubled crypto environment could negatively impact the company through a less profitable mining industry.

Finally, Soluna stock could present extreme volatility.

Takeaway

Taking everything into account, Soluna is a micro-cap company with a bright idea: developing green data centers using surplus energy from renewable power parks. Following the recent rally fueled by the AI hype, the stock price has significantly corrected, making Soluna a more attractive investment now. Despite the AI hype lifting valuations, the data center ecosystem is here to stay and grow. Soluna has already been operating projects while planning to land others in the coming years and grow significantly. Provided management successfully executes its business plan, the company will justify a significantly higher valuation in the future. However, risks are not absent, and investors should acknowledge that fact. Thus, investors with a long-term perspective should consider initiating a position at current or lower levels, but be prepared to face potential challenges and volatility, and perhaps save some bullets for the future.

data.title: Floating Rates: Capturing Short-Term Yields As The Yield Curve Normalizes
data.description:
With the yield curve normalized and rates easing, now is the time for investors to seek higher yield and stability through floating rate notes and diversified income strategies.

After two years of inversion, the yield curve has normalized. On October 31, 2025, the 2s/10s spread closed at +0.49% following the late October policy cut, with longer-maturity yields remaining elevated as markets reassess inflation and Treasury supply conditions. The Fed lowered the policy rate to 3.75%–4.00% on October 29 and indicated it will halt balance-sheet runoff on December 1.

Investors, however, continue to sit on record levels of cash with money market fund assets at approximately $7 trillion. While cash has been a safe rate-sensitive haven, it provides limited upside once policy rates peak. With the curve only modestly positive, a rate-cutting cycle that is expected to be shallow, and continued pressure on long-term bond yields, investment-grade floating-rate notes (FRNs) remain a compelling short-duration income option.

The Curve Continues to Normalize

The steepening marks a transition from the “higher-for-longer” stance that defined 2023 - 2024. However, the slow pace and shallow magnitude of rate cuts have continued to make floating rate an attractive option within an income portfolio. Market-implied expectations point to modest, maybe 2 or 3, additional cuts through 2026, contingent on data. If the labor market softens faster, easing could come sooner; if growth holds, cuts may be delayed. Elevated inflation, widening fiscal deficits and geopolitical tension have kept upward pressure on long-term yields even as the Fed cuts rates.

Difference Between 10-Year and 2-Year US Treasury Yields

For investors, this environment challenges traditional playbooks. Long-duration bonds, which prospered during decades of falling yields, now offer limited price appreciation and heightened downside if yields rise again. The recent volatility in Treasuries reflects shifting expectations for inflation and policy. Extending duration preemptively could prove costly should long yields remain stubbornly high.

Why Floating Rate Notes Still Make Sense

We believe that these factors continue to support an allocation to the short end of the yield where FRNs may provide a way to earn attractive income while minimizing exposure to rate volatility.

Floating rate notes, such as the VanEck IG Floating Rate ETF (FLTR), offer an efficient way to navigate today’s shifting rate environment. FRNs pay coupons that reset, usually quarterly, based on SOFR plus fixed spread, giving them near-zero duration and insulating prices from rate swings.

At the end of October, IG FRNs yielded 5.12%, comparing favorably with short- and fixed-rate corporate bonds (3.82% and 4.82%, respectively). Because their coupons adjust with market rates, FRNs exhibit low price sensitivity across both tightening and easing cycles. This structure helps reduce mark-to-market volatility while preserving income potential.

In short, FRNs capture today’s elevated short-term yields without adding duration risk. If short-term rates decline, coupon income adjusts lower, but prices typically remain stable, a balanced trade-off between income and capital stability.

Building Resilience Through Diversification

To further strengthen an ultrashort duration approach, investors can pair FRNs with exposure to CLOs through VanEck CLO ETF (CLOI) and VanEck AA–BB CLO ETF (CLOB). CLOs are portfolios of senior secured loans with floating coupons and structural credit protection.

  • CLOI focuses on IG tranches which were yielding yields 4.95% and have low credit risk. Notably, no investment grade CLO has ever defaulted post GFC.
  • CLOB primarily holds AA–BB rated tranches which recently offered yields of 6.82%.

CLO structures also include built-in protections such as diversification across hundreds of loans and priority in payment waterfalls. These features have helped maintain strong performance through past cycles. For investors seeking to maintain income without extending duration, CLOs represent an attractive complement to corporate FRNs.

data.title: The Paradox Of Quantum Narratives: When Markets Collapse The Wave Function
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The Paradox of Quantum Narratives

Quantum mechanics has always delighted in paradoxes: the cat that’s both alive and dead, the particle that is also a wave. So perhaps it’s fitting that the world of quantum investing would take on a similar ambiguity. In October, reports surfaced suggesting that the Trump administration was in active discussions to take equity stakes in pure-play quantum computing firms like D-Wave (QBTS), IonQ (IONQ) and Rigetti (RGTI). Hours later, another report denied that any such talks were occurring1. Like a superposition2, both statements seemed true until observed, and neither confirmed anything definitive. The mere possibility, however, was enough to send these quantum names surging by double digits in a single trading session.3

Between State Capitalism and Market Faith

Whether or not Washington ever takes those stakes may matter less than what the rumors represent. We’re witnessing a fusion of industrial policy and market psychology unlike anything since the post-war era. The Barron’s cover story on “How Trump Sparked a New Era of State Capitalism”4 frames it bluntly: Uncle Sam has become one of the most influential investors in America, converting subsidies and grants into equity positions across sectors from chips to minerals. The goal is dual: fortify supply chains and project national resilience. But in practice, each intervention rewrites the rules of private enterprise. Investors are suddenly learning to price political intent as a new risk premium or opportunity.

The Intel Case Study: Confidence as Capital

Intel (INTC) offers the clearest recent precedent. Once a symbol of American decline in semiconductors, it became a beneficiary of this new policy wave: A government grant turned into a 10% equity stake, followed by private inflows from SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) and NVIDIA (NVDA). The outcome wasn’t just liquidity; it was legitimacy. Markets re-rated Intel not because of immediate profit improvement, but because a credible buyer of last resort had stepped in. The Trump stake bought Intel time, and time bought Intel confidence.5 In a capital-intensive business where psychology often leads fundamentals, that confidence can be worth tens of billions.

The Stakes for Quantum’s Future

The quantum computing sector, still years away from commercial scale, now sits at the intersection of technology’s most speculative promise and the state’s most strategic ambitions. If Intel’s revival showed that a government stake can restore belief, the quantum paradox6 shows that even rumors of involvement can reprice entire industries. The outcome, whether the talks were real, denied or both, illustrates how markets are beginning to trade not just on the physics of qubits7, but on the politics of capital allocation. In that sense, quantum may be the perfect metaphor for this new industrial era: everything is uncertain, until it isn’t.

From Paradox to Portfolio

For investors, the task now is to separate the narrative superposition from measurable progress. The four public quantum names, D-Wave, IonQ, Rigetti and Quantum Computing Inc. (QUBT), occupy a strange position in markets: companies with negligible revenues, immense optionality and capitalization structures that trade more on faith than fundamentals. They’ve each become proxies for different visions of quantum’s future, hardware, software and hybrid approaches to algorithmic acceleration. In that sense, they are not yet competing with one another so much as competing for belief: which firm can convince investors, partners and perhaps governments that their qubits will collapse the probability wave8 first. Whether state capital enters the picture or not, the real investment question may be whether confidence alone can sustain a quantum ecosystem long enough for the technology to prove itself.

As we see in Figure 1:

  • Quantum computing equities, particularly Rigetti and D-Wave, surged immediately following Google’s December 2024 unveiling of its Willow quantum chip, reflecting renewed investor belief in near-term commercial viability. IonQ and Quantum Computing Inc. participated modestly, but the market clearly favored firms perceived to benefit from spillover technological validation and rising strategic interest in quantum hardware.
  • By mid-2025, another sharp rally emerged, led by Rigetti and D-Wave, coinciding with announcements around quantum-AI convergence and geopolitical funding in quantum R&D. The figure illustrates the extreme sensitivity to sentiment and policy news. The late-2025 retracement suggests investors, again, were differentiating between tangible progress and narrative momentum.

In our discussions with investors, we note that it’s far easier to write catchy headlines than it is to remember that the process to get to universal, fault-tolerate quantum computing9 will take a series of advances occurring over the coming years.

Figure 1: Performance of the Four Public Quantum Companies since Google’s Willow Chip Announcement in 2024

Sources: WisdomTree, Bloomberg and, for Google’s Willow chip announcement date, Google Research, “Meet Willow, our state-of-the-art quantum chip” [blog post], Google, 12/9/24. Past performance is not indicative of future returns.

Capturing the Quantum Computing Ecosystem

At WisdomTree, we’ve designed our quantum computing methodology to systematically identify and weight the companies driving progress across the full stack of this emerging field, from the physics of qubits to the commercialization of cloud-based quantum services. The WisdomTree Quantum Computing Fund (WQTM) is the specific strategy reflecting this process.10

From Classification to Relevance

Each company is assigned a Relevancy Score, ranging from 1 to 3, reflecting the significance of its quantum computing involvement and its impact on the field’s advancement. Importantly, what we note below are guidelines to help investors understand what we mean when we say the relevancy score, but we are recognizing as well that quantum computing is a fast-moving space. If new details and important focal points are coming to light as quantum computing evolves, our framework is flexible enough to account for this in the future. We look at how what’s important or impactful in quantum computing is evolving, as well as what different companies are doing and how that may be evolving.

  • A Relevancy Score of 3 identifies companies at the core of the quantum revolution: those directly developing qubits, chips or algorithms (e.g., IonQ, Rigetti, D-Wave).
  • Relevancy 2 captures those in adjacent or foundational segments, such as post-quantum cryptography or quantum networking.
  • Relevancy 1 applies to indirect enablers, such as high-performance computing providers or diversified firms supplying quantum-ready infrastructure.

This layered approach ensures the portfolio remains anchored in the technology’s frontier, while acknowledging the broader ecosystem that will make large-scale, universal, fault-tolerant quantum computing viable. In the latter part of 2025, we recognize the difference between what might provide utility in the near term, and what might be more geared toward the future fault-tolerant era in this space.

Purity: Differentiating Core from Complementary Players

Alongside Relevancy, every company is tagged as Pure or Diversified, based on business focus and quantum revenue concentration.

  • Pure players are firms whose identity and growth trajectory are predominantly tied to quantum computing innovation, whether hardware (D-Wave, Rigetti), software (Quantum Computing Inc.) or hybrid systems (IonQ).
  • Diversified players are established technology leaders such as IBM, Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), whose quantum initiatives sit within larger, multi-segment operations but contribute crucially to ecosystem development. We recognize that they are one of the driving forces behind some of the progress we are seeing overall in quantum computing.

This Purity dimension balances exposure: pure plays drive thematic precision and upside potential, while diversified leaders provide the potential for liquidity and volatility mitigation.

Weighting the Ecosystem

Once relevancy and purity are established, our methodology applies a two-stage weighting process. Equal-weighted starting positions are first adjusted upward for higher Relevancy Scores and then further scaled by Purity, giving pure and highly relevant firms the largest representation in the final Index. The outcome is a quantitatively disciplined yet thematically expressive portfolio, where index weights reflect not market cap dominance but quantum significance.

Visible in Figure 2, as of the most recent rebalance in August 2025, the top 10 holdings represent 43.1% of the total Index, led by D-Wave, IonQ and Rigetti, each receiving the highest possible Relevancy and Purity designations. This structure helps ensure that if the market re-rates the sector, whether from government partnership rumors or real technical milestones, WisdomTree investors are positioned to capture the potential upside.

Figure 2: Top 10 Positions as of the Most Recent Rebalancing (August 2025)

Sources: WisdomTree, Classiq, with data shown as of the August 2025 rebalancing process. Subject to change.

Conclusion

Quantum computing today sits in a peculiar state of duality; its commercial impact remains years away, yet its strategic importance is already undeniable. Governments debate whether to invest; investors debate whether to believe. And while both sides toggle between speculation and conviction, the real progress continues in laboratories, data centers and chip foundries across the world. The companies at the heart of this movement - those engineering qubits11, building error-correction algorithms and designing quantum operating systems - are shaping an infrastructure that will one day be as indispensable as semiconductors or cloud computing. The paradox is that you don’t wait for quantum computing to be certain before it becomes investable; you position for its inevitability while uncertainty is still priced in.

That’s precisely what WisdomTree’s methodology is designed to do. By combining quantitative discipline with qualitative insight, balancing relevancy, purity and ecosystem breadth, we’ve constructed a framework that is designed to capture the economic nucleus of quantum progress, not just its headlines. The outcome is an exposure that naturally tilts toward the innovators most likely to define the field’s future, while maintaining diversified ballast in enabling technologies and adjacent infrastructure. If the sector rallies, whether from a government catalyst, technical breakthrough or sudden re-rating of possibility, our structure helps ensure participation with purpose. In an industry where timelines stretch across decades, WisdomTree’s approach is about more than capturing returns; it’s about securing a front-row seat to the next computational epoch.

____________

1 Sources: The Wall Street Journal Staff, “Trump administration in talks to take equity stakes in quantum computing firms,” The Wall Street Journal, 10/22/25; M. Tatananni and A. Clark, “Trump administration denies negotiating stakes in quantum computing companies: These stocks leaped on the news,” Barron’s, 10/23/25.

2 Superposition is the quantum principle that a qubit can occupy multiple states at once - such as 0 and 1 - until it is measured. This property allows quantum computers to explore many possibilities in parallel.

3 Source: M. Tatananni and A. Clark, 10/23/25.

4 Source: J. Light, “How Trump sparked a new era of state capitalism,” Barron’s, 10/22/25.

5 Source: M. Acton, “Intel shares jump on improved revenue as turnaround shows progress,” Financial Times, 10/23/25.

6 A quantum paradox is an apparent contradiction that arises when quantum behavior defies classical intuition, revealing how quantum systems operate differently from everyday physics. These paradoxes often highlight foundational limits of measurement, determinism, and observation.

7 Qubits are the basic units of quantum information that can exist in a combination of 0 and 1 simultaneously. They enable quantum computers to process information in ways impossible for classical bits.

8 A probability wave (or wavefunction) describes the mathematical distribution of all possible states a quantum system can occupy. Measuring the system collapses this wave into a single outcome.

9 Refers to methods that allow quantum computers to operate reliably even when individual qubits and operations experience errors. It relies on quantum error-correcting codes to ensure stable, scalable computation.

10 WQTM is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Classiq Quantum Computing Index.

11 The physical hardware implementations of qubits - such as superconducting circuits or trapped ions - that embody the idealized quantum information unit in real devices. They come with real-world constraints like noise, decoherence, and fabrication limits.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006.
